intro_band.agent_remit_summary: yaah is a local developer command-line tool that generates coding-agent configuration for Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI from a single Go source of truth, and ships a built-in security-and-quality toolset alongside it. Its central promise is that every generated agent configuration carries the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a catastrophic-command guard, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger — plus a built-in MCP server (yaah serve) and a per-session audit log. yaah may contact only the local developer, the configured LLM provider, the context7 and pulumi MCP servers, its own built-in MCP server, and the coding-agent plugin marketplace; catastrophic shell commands must be blocked before they run and remote MCP servers must be reached over TLS.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Go 1.25 CLI (cmd/yaah, cobra) built on the Interface to Registry to Generator pattern. pkg/harness wires hook handlers, MCP/LSP providers, skills, agents, commands and a session.Store into a Harness; pkg/hooks/handlers holds the five protective handlers (Linter, CommandGuard, SecretScanner, CommentChecker, SessionLogger); pkg/generator emits per-agent config via four AgentGenerator implementations. yaah hook <event> is the runtime dispatcher invoked by the generated agent's hook config; yaah serve runs a built-in MCP server exposing seven tools. The hook handlers fire only for events the target agent's generator actually emits — and the Codex generator maps PreToolUse/PostToolUse to the empty string, so the protective hooks are silently absent from Codex-targeted configuration.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy-implementation divergence on the harness's single central promise. The remit states that every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same protective hook set; the code does not deliver this. pkg/generator/hookmap.go has no Codex entry for PreToolUse or PostToolUse, so CodexGenerator.GenerateHooks() emits a .codex/hooks.json containing only SessionStart and Stop — the command guard, secret scanner, linter, and comment checker are dropped for the Codex target, and TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported codifies that drop as expected behaviour.

A second theme runs through the protections that do exist: they are advisory or post-hoc rather than enforcing. The secret scanner and linter are PostToolUse handlers that run after the write has already landed, the yaah_check_command MCP tool only returns a safe/unsafe verdict with no blocking, and there is no human-approval checkpoint for file writes or for write/send/execute MCP tools — only catastrophic bash commands are hard-blocked, and only via a small bypassable regex denylist.

rules.rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger.

Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.
Remote MCP servers MUST be reached over TLS.

MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.
Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.
Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.
High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.
The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.
The MCP server configuration and the per-agent configuration files MUST stay consistent with each other.
Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.
A dangerous shell command caught by the command guard is blocked, not run, and the block is recorded to the session audit log.
Secret-scan, lint, and comment findings are recorded to the session audit log and surfaced to the operator.

The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.

findings.summary: Codex-targeted configuration ships without the protective Pre/PostToolUse hooks — the command guard, secret scanner, linter, and comment checker are silently dropped.
findings.description: The remit's central promise is that every generated agent configuration carries the same protective hook set. pkg/generator/hookmap.go defines no Codex event name for HookPreToolUse or HookPostToolUse, so CodexGenerator.GenerateHooks() emits a .codex/hooks.json containing only SessionStart and Stop. A developer who runs yaah generate --agent codex receives a configuration with no command guard, no secret scanner, no linter, and no comment checker — only the session logger survives. TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported codifies this drop as expected behaviour.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger.

evidence.snippet: schema.HookPreToolUse mapping has Claude/OpenCode/Copilot entries but no Codex field, so CodexEventName(HookPreToolUse) returns ""
schema.HookPostToolUse mapping likewise has no Codex field
GenerateHooks skips any event whose CodexEventName is empty, dropping PreToolUse/PostToolUse from .codex/hooks.json
TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported asserts hooks data is nil when only PreToolUse is configured — the drop is tested-in as expected behaviour

recommended_actions: Add Codex event names for PreToolUse and PostToolUse in pkg/generator/hookmap.go (Codex hooks.json supports them) so the protective handlers reach the Codex target.
If Codex genuinely cannot run Pre/PostToolUse hooks, make yaah generate fail loudly for the codex target rather than silently emitting a degraded config, and document the protection gap.

findings.summary: No human-approval checkpoint exists for file writes or for write/send/execute MCP tools — only catastrophic bash commands are blocked.
findings.description: The remit requires destructive shell commands, file writes, and write/send/execute MCP tools to reach a human checkpoint before they run. The harness blocks catastrophic bash via CommandGuard (PreToolUse) and post-hoc flags secrets in written files, but there is no interposition that pauses a file write or a write/send/execute tool call for human review. yaah_planning_init writes to the workspace with no gate.
findings.policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.

evidence.snippet: SecretScanner runs on PostToolUse — i.e. after the Edit/Write has already landed on disk; no pre-write gate exists
yaah_planning_init writes STATE.md and creates directories with no approval or confirmation step
CommandGuard.Execute blocks only Bash commands; Edit/Write/MultiEdit have no PreToolUse gate

recommended_actions: Add a PreToolUse gate for Edit/Write/MultiEdit that requires explicit confirmation, or document that approval is delegated to the host agent's permission system and verify each generated config enables it.
Gate the write-capable MCP tool (yaah_planning_init) behind a confirmation or a dry-run/confirm two-step.

findings.summary: Hardcoded-credential detection is post-write only — the secret scanner runs on PostToolUse, after the edit has already been written to disk.
findings.description: The remit requires hardcoded credentials in edited files to be detected before the change is accepted. SecretScanner is a PostToolUse handler: it scans the file after Edit/Write/MultiEdit has completed. It can block the action being marked successful and surface a remediation message, but the credential is already on disk and may already be committable. Detection is line-by-line regex with a fixed pattern set, so multi-line or novel secret formats are missed.
findings.policy_rule_text: Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.

evidence.snippet: Events() returns HookPostToolUse only — the scanner cannot run before the write
ScanFile reads the file from disk line-by-line after the write has completed
fixed regex pattern set; no entropy-based or multi-line detection — novel credential formats are missed

recommended_actions: Treat PostToolUse blocking as detection-after-write and document that the file is already on disk; recommend the operator add a pre-commit secret scan as the actual before-accepted gate.
Add an entropy-based fallback pattern to catch credential formats not in the fixed list.

findings.summary: No adversarial testing of the protections, no threat model, and no security-disclosure process.
findings.description: The remit asks the project to run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries its claimed protections. The repo has unit tests for individual handlers and generators but no end-to-end adversarial fixture, no SECURITY.md, and no threat-model document. The Codex hook-drop is exactly the class of bug an end-to-end carry-the-protections test would have caught.
findings.policy_rule_text: The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.

evidence.snippet: No SECURITY.md and no threat-model document found anywhere in the repository
Generator tests assert structural shape but never assert that a generated config end-to-end carries the full protective hook set for every target

recommended_actions: Add an end-to-end test that generates config for every target and asserts the linter, command guard, secret scanner, and comment checker are all present and wired.
Publish a SECURITY.md with a disclosure process and a short threat model covering the harness's protective promises.

findings.summary: No control prevents poisoning writes into agent-managed, session-loaded files (AGENTS.md, generated configs, .planning/STATE.md).
findings.description: The remit forbids agent-managed session-loaded files from being writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed. yaah maintains exactly such files — the generated AGENTS.md / settings.json loaded at session start, and .planning/STATE.md written by the yaah_planning_init MCP tool and read back by yaah_planning_status. Nothing in the harness restricts or reviews writes into these files: the secret scanner and comment checker run on edits but neither detects an injected instruction, and there is no allowlist of which files may be modified. A generated config or AGENTS.md edited by injected content re-enters context on the next session.
findings.policy_rule_text: Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.

evidence.snippet: yaah_planning_init writes .planning/STATE.md, which is later read back by yaah_planning_status at tools.go:302
CommentChecker inspects edits for placeholder/non-English comments only — it does not detect injected instructions in session-loaded files
Root AGENTS.md is explicitly Managed by agent and loaded at session start, with no write-protection control in the harness

recommended_actions: Add a handler or generator-side check that flags or blocks writes into agent-context files (AGENTS.md, generated settings, .planning/STATE.md) for human review.
Document which files are session-loaded and treat edits to them as requiring approval.

findings.summary: Third-party MCP servers are launched via npx -y, which auto-installs the latest unpinned package on every run.
findings.description: The remit's Known Good Baseline requires third-party MCP servers to be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version, with no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run. The context7 provider (and the notion provider) emit a stdio command of npx -y @context7/mcp / npx -y @notionhq/notion-mcp-server. npx -y resolves and installs the latest published version each invocation with no version pin and no integrity check — exactly the auto-install-unpinned-on-every-run pattern the remit forbids. A compromised or rug-pulled upstream package would be executed with the developer's permissions.

tags.label: Tool definitions are signed and version-pinned

findings.policy_rule_text: Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.

evidence.snippet: Server() returns Command npx with Args [-y, @context7/mcp] — no version pin and no integrity check
Server() returns Command npx with Args [-y, @notionhq/notion-mcp-server] — no version pin
Generated project MCP config carries the same unpinned npx -y @context7/mcp invocation

recommended_actions: Pin third-party MCP packages to an exact version (e.g. @context7/mcp@<version>) and ideally an integrity hash, in the provider definitions under pkg/mcp/providers/.
Document a review/update process for bumping pinned MCP server versions.

findings.summary: The catastrophic-command guard is a small regex denylist that is trivially bypassable.
findings.description: CommandGuard blocks seven hard-coded regex patterns. The denylist is narrow: rm -fr /, rm --recursive --force /, env-var-indirected paths, and git push -f origin main (short flag — the regex requires the literal --force) are not matched. This is a deny-list with the Whac-A-Mole property: it raises the bar but does not deliver the remit's MUST-be-blocked guarantee.
findings.policy_rule_text: Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.

evidence.snippet: NewCommandGuard registers exactly 7 fixed regex patterns
Force-push pattern requires the literal --force; git push -f origin main is not matched
Pattern rm -rf / does not match rm -fr / or rm --recursive --force /

recommended_actions: Broaden the guard patterns to cover short-flag and reordered-flag variants (-fr, -f, --recursive --force).
Alternatively, document the guard as best-effort defence-in-depth and soften the remit language from MUST-block to a layered control.

findings.summary: Lint and comment findings are surfaced to the operator but not recorded to the structured session audit log.
findings.description: The remit requires secret-scan, lint, and comment findings to be recorded to the session audit log. The session.Session struct has a Findings field, but HandleHookEvent never populates it — it records ToolCallRecord and BlockedCalls only. Handler outputs are written to stdout/stderr and, when a handler blocks, the Reason is captured on the BlockedCall, but a non-blocking lint or comment finding leaves no durable structured record.
findings.policy_rule_text: Secret-scan, lint, and comment findings are recorded to the session audit log and surfaced to the operator.

evidence.snippet: Session.Findings []Finding field is declared in the schema
HandleHookEvent updates ToolCalls and BlockedCalls but never appends to sess.Findings
Non-blocking lint output is returned as Result.Output and printed to stdout, not persisted as a session Finding

recommended_actions: In HandleHookEvent, parse handler Results into session.Finding records and append them to sess.Findings before Save, so lint/comment/secret findings are durably recorded.

findings.summary: Generated per-agent configurations diverge from each other — the Codex target omits hooks the other targets carry.
findings.description: The remit requires the MCP server configuration and the per-agent configuration files to stay consistent. Because the Codex generator drops Pre/PostToolUse hooks, the set of protections in .codex/hooks.json is not consistent with .claude/settings.json or the Copilot hooks.json. A developer comparing two generated configs sees different protection sets with no warning. This is the consistency facet of the hookmap defect.
findings.policy_rule_text: The MCP server configuration and the per-agent configuration files MUST stay consistent with each other. A change to one must not silently leave the other stale.

evidence.snippet: hookMappings produces per-agent event-name sets that differ; the Codex column lacks PreToolUse and PostToolUse
Codex GenerateHooks emits a strict subset of events relative to claude.go / copilot.go

recommended_actions: Make generation emit a manifest or warning listing which protections each target received, so divergence is visible.
Fix the underlying hookmap gap (see PRAX-2026-05-19-001).

findings.summary: A notion MCP provider exists in the codebase but is not in the remit's authorized counterparty / configured-servers list.
findings.description: The remit authorizes only context7, pulumi, and the built-in yaah MCP server, and states any counterparty not listed is unauthorized by default. pkg/mcp/providers/notion.go defines a fully-functional Notion MCP provider; NewWithDefaults registers it whenever opts.NotionToken is non-empty. AllDefaults() does not set NotionToken, so the default yaah binary does not ship it — but the capability is present and one config value away from adding an undeclared external counterparty (Notion's API).
findings.policy_rule_text: Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.

evidence.snippet: Notion provider Server() targets the Notion API via @notionhq/notion-mcp-server — a counterparty not in the remit
NewWithDefaults registers the Notion provider when opts.NotionToken is non-empty

recommended_actions: Either add Notion to the remit's authorized counterparties, or remove the Notion provider / gate it behind an explicit opt-in documented as out-of-remit.

positives.title: Pinned, reviewable Go dependency set
positives.description: go.mod with a committed go.sum pins every dependency to an exact version and hash; the tree is small (8 modules total) and reviewable.

positives.title: SHA-pinned CI actions and signed releases
positives.description: Every GitHub Action in ci.yaml is pinned to a commit SHA with a version comment; GoReleaser performs cosign signing and SBOM generation on release.

positives.title: Durable structured session audit trail
positives.description: session.Store persists per-session JSON capturing tool calls, blocked calls, files modified, and findings, using an atomic temp-file-plus-rename write.

positives.title: Session ID path-traversal hardening
positives.description: sanitizeID rejects path separators and relative components before a session ID is used as a filename, preventing directory traversal in the store.

positives.title: Specific, verifiable Worker Remit
positives.description: The remit names exact tools, counterparties, and forbidden actions, which makes code-level verification of each rule possible.

positives.title: Built-in MCP tool inventory matches the authorized list
positives.description: The seven tools registered by the built-in MCP server match the remit's authorized built-in MCP tool list exactly, with no undeclared tools.

rows.purpose: Records session start/end/stop events with session ID and cwd
Records tool calls, blocked calls, files modified, and handler findings for each session

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. yaah is a well-structured, narrowly-scoped CLI with pinned Go dependencies, SHA-pinned CI, and a durable structured session audit trail — genuine strengths in supply chain, domain, and monitoring. But its security posture is dragged down by Zero Trust: the protective controls it ships are advisory and post-hoc rather than enforcing, there is no approval checkpoint for writes or write/send tool calls, and the harness's own central promise — uniform protective hooks across every generated agent — is broken for the Codex target. The absence of any adversarial testing of those protections leaves the gap both exploitable and unverified.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: yaah is a narrow CLI with no LLM and no unbounded tool surface in the harness itself; the built-in MCP tool set matches the remit's authorized list exactly. Minor: a notion MCP provider exists in code though it is not a remit-authorized counterparty.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The harness does not ingest external content into an LLM context; it generates static config. The one gap is that agent-context files (AGENTS.md, .planning/STATE.md) are written without integrity protection.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: The protections are real but post-hoc and non-enforcing: the secret scanner and linter are PostToolUse handlers that run after the write, yaah_check_command is advisory only, there is no approval gate for writes or write/send MCP tools, and the Codex target ships with no Pre/PostToolUse protections at all.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: go.mod plus a committed go.sum pin all 8 dependencies and CI pins every Action SHA with cosign signing and SBOM. The gap is that third-party MCP servers are launched via npx -y, auto-installing the latest unpinned package on every run.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Unit tests exist for handlers and generators but there is no adversarial testing, no SECURITY.md, no threat model, and no end-to-end test confirming a generated config actually carries the protections it claims — which is why the Codex hook-drop went uncaught.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: SessionLogger writes timestamped events and session.Store persists structured per-session JSON with atomic writes — durable and structured. Gaps: the logs are gitignored so not change-audited, and the declared Session.Findings field is never populated by the harness.
